paper_title: On-Policy Adversarial Flow Distillation for Autoregressive Video Generation

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Autoregressive video generators are attractive for streaming, long-horizon, and interactive applications, but distilling strong black-box teachers into causal students remains difficult. The student must learn under its own rollout distribution, whereas practical teachers may expose only prompt-conditioned completed videos and may differ in architecture, capacity, temporal design, and sampling schedule. This interface makes supervised fine-tuning off-policy, score-based distillation inapplicable, and direct adversarial imitation too sparse for denoising-time credit assignment. We propose Adversarial Flow Distillation (AFD), an on-policy framework for heterogeneous black-box video distillation. AFD queries the teacher and rolls out the current student on the same prompts, trains a prompt-paired Bradley-Terry discriminator to estimate clean-sample teacher-student discrepancy, and converts the resulting on-policy advantage into forward-process flow-matching updates on the student's own noised states. Thus, AFD provides dense velocity-field supervision while requiring no teacher scores, latents, denoising trajectories, step alignment, or reverse-chain reinforcement learning. Experiments across two causal AR student families show that AFD consistently improves motion- and physics-sensitive generation while preserving general video quality, and ablations validate the importance of adaptive on-policy feedback and forward-process credit assignment. The method requires only clean teacher videos and student rollouts, providing a practical route for distilling proprietary or heterogeneous video generators into efficient autoregressive students.

sections.title: Start
sections.content: On-Policy Adversarial Flow Distillation for Autoregressive Video Generation Yang Luo1, Shengju Qian2, Xiaohang Tang3, Zirui Zhu1, Yong Liu1, Xin Wang2, Yang You1 1National Univesity of Singapore 2LIGHTSPEED 3University College London # yang_luo@u.nus.edu Corresponding Author 2026-05-26 Abstract Autoregressive video generators are attractive for streaming, long-horizon, and interactive applications, but distilling strong black-box teachers into causal students remains difficult. The student must learn under its own rollout distribution, whereas practical teachers may expose only promptconditioned completed videos and may differ in architecture, capacity, temporal design, and sampling schedule. This interface makes supervised fine-tuning off-policy, score-based distillation inapplicable, and direct adversarial imitation too sparse for denoising-time credit assignment. We propose Adversarial Flow Distillation (AFD), an on-policy framework for heterogeneous black-box video distillation. AFD queries the teacher and rolls out the current student on the same prompts, trains prompt-paired BradleyTerry discriminator to estimate clean-sample teacherstudent discrepancy, and converts the resulting on-policy advantage into forward-process flow-matching updates on the students own noised states. Thus, AFD provides dense velocity-field supervision while requiring no teacher scores, latents, denoising trajectories, step alignment, or reverse-chain reinforcement learning. Experiments across two causal AR student families show that AFD consistently improves motionand physics-sensitive generation while preserving general video quality, and ablations validate the importance of adaptive on-policy feedback and forward-process credit assignment. The method requires only clean teacher videos and student rollouts, providing practical route for distilling proprietary or heterogeneous video generators into efficient autoregressive students. 1. Introduction Efficient deployment of modern video generators increasingly depends on distilling large diffusion, score-based, or flow-matching models into smaller students [Chern et al., 2025, Ho et al., 2020, Kim et al., 2025, Lipman et al., 2023, Liu et al., 2022, Salimans and Ho, 2022, Song et al., 2021, 2023, Yin et al., 2024]. In practice, many strong video teachers are accessible only as black-box samplers that return completed clips, without exposing scores, logits, latents, sampler states, or denoising trajectories [Brooks et al., 2024, Kling Team, 2025, Polyak et al., 2024]. This is especially restrictive for autoregressive (AR) students such as Self-Forcing [Huang et al., 2025]: black-box teacher may use different architecture, temporal conditioning scheme, and long denoising schedule, while the deployable AR student generates causally with few denoising steps. Existing distillation recipes therefore lack an aligned supervision channel from the teachers hidden generation process to the students rollout distribution and intermediate noised states. straightforward adaptation strategy is supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on teacher-generated videos. Although SFT does not require teacher scores, it trains the AR student under teacher-induced prefixes rather than under the students own rollout distribution. At inference time, the student conditions on its previously generated frames; local errors can therefore shift future denoising states and accumulate over the video horizon [Bengio et al., 2015]. preliminary SFT sweep in Figure 1 confirms this mismatch: longer off-policy training fails to consistently improve either VBench [Huang et al., 2024] or VideoPhy-2 [Bansal et al., 2026]. The SFT mismatch motivates on-policy adaptation, yet existing on-policy objectives still assume 6 2 0 2 5 2 ] . [ 1 5 0 1 6 2 . 5 0 6 2 : r On-Policy Adversarial Flow Distillation for Autoregressive Video Generation supervision unavailable in black-box video distillation. DMD-style objectives require teacher scores, density ratios, or compatible noised states [Yin et al., 2024], none of which are available from sampling-only video teacher. Rewardand preference-based diffusion alignment can optimize models from sample-level feedback [Black et al., 2023, Liu et al., 2025a,b, Wallace et al., 2024, Xue et al., 2025], but scalar score on completed video does not identify which frames, motion patterns, or denoising states account for the teacherstudent discrepancy. The useful signal is observable only at completed videos, while the object being trained is time-dependent vector field evaluated on the students own noised AR states. The key challenge is therefore not merely to assign reward to video, but to lift black-box video evidence into dense flow-matching supervision for the students causal denoising process. We propose Adversarial Flow Distillation (AFD), an on-policy distillation framework for autoregressive video generation under heterogeneous black-box teacher access. AFD factorizes the problem into clean-sample distribution-ratio estimation and forward-process vector-field regression. For each prompt batch, it queries the teacher for completed videos, rolls out the current student under autoregressive selfconditioning, and trains prompt-conditioned discriminator on teacher samples versus current student samples. The discriminator produces an on-policy advantage score, normalized against the current batch or prompt distribution, which is identifiable from black-box samples and coevolves with the student. Figure 1 Preliminary SFT trend for an AR video student. VBench and VideoPhy-2 scores decrease rather than improve monotonically as SFT steps increase. AFD then uses this advantage inside reward-weighted flow-matching objective rather than reversetrajectory policy-gradient objective. Student rollouts are forward-noised by the students own schedule, producing the same kind of intermediate states on which its velocity field is trained. Within each on-policy batch, high-advantage rollouts form positive examples and low-advantage rollouts form negative examples; the student is then trained to move its vector field toward the forward velocity of higher-scoring rollouts and away from lower-scoring rollouts, with the discriminator advantage controlling the strength of this contrastive correction. This turns black-box video-level signal into denoising-time updates on the students own noised states. Consequently, AFD requires no teacher scores, no teacherstudent step alignment, and no stored reverse trajectories, while still providing dense supervision beyond video-level adversarial training. Our contributions are as follows. We identify black-box heterogeneous on-policy distillation as core obstacle for autoregressive video students, and show that off-policy SFT, score-based DMD, and direct video-level adversarial training are mismatched to the limited teacher interface. We introduce Adversarial Flow Distillation (AFD), score-free distillation framework that estimates teacherstudent discrepancy from completed videos and converts it into dense forward-process flow-matching updates on the students own noised rollouts. We evaluate AFD on two causal autoregressive video backbones, showing consistent gains on motionand physics-sensitive metrics under black-box teacher access, together with ablations on domain adaptation and discriminator design. 2 On-Policy Adversarial Flow Distillation for Autoregressive Video Generation 2. Related Work Video diffusion and flow models. DDPM [Ho et al., 2020] and score-based SDEs [Song et al., 2021] established denoising-time generation, while Flow Matching [Lipman et al., 2023] and Rectified Flow [Liu et al., 2022] recast generation as continuous-time vector-field learning. DiT [Peebles and Xie, 2023] improved transformer diffusion scalability and now underlies video systems including Video Diffusion Models [Ho et al., 2022], Lumiere [Bar-Tal et al., 2024], CogVideoX [Yang et al., 2024], Movie Gen [Polyak et al., 2024], Sora [Brooks et al., 2024], Kling-Omni [Kling Team, 2025], and Wan [Team Wan et al., 2025]. Recent video distillation work such as V.I.P. [Kim et al., 2025] and LiveTalk [Chern et al., 2025] studies online or on-policy recipes for efficient video generation. AFD focuses on different transfer interface: an AR student learns on its own rollouts while sampling-only teacher provides only completed videos. Black-box on-policy distillation. On-policy distillation [Lu and Thinking Machines Lab, 2025, Ye et al., 2025] keeps training on the students own trajectory distribution while using teacher for supervision. In language models, this often means teacher feedback on student-generated prefixes, and Rethinking OPD [Li et al., 2026b] analyzes when such feedback succeeds or fails. VLA-OPD [Zhong et al., 2026] and Video-OPD [Li et al., 2026a] apply the same on-policy idea to action and temporal grounding tasks. Our setting differs from LLM OPD because the teacher cannot provide token-level logits or local probability ratios: sampling-only video teacher returns completed clips, while the student must learn continuous-time video flow. AFD therefore estimates teacherstudent distributional discrepancy with discriminator and projects that signal to denoising-time states with DiffusionNFT. Adversarial and preference-guided alignment. DDPO [Black et al., 2023], Diffusion-DPO [Wallace et al., 2024], and VideoAlign [Liu et al., 2025b] show that learned or human feedback can guide diffusion models, but reverse-trajectory policy gradients are expensive for video. DiffusionNFT [Zheng et al., 2025] instead optimizes on the forward process from clean generated samples, avoiding likelihood estimation and reverse-trajectory storage; Astrolabe [Zhang et al., 2026] adapts this view to distilled AR video alignment. Continuous Adversarial Flow Models [Lin et al., 2026] further suggest that learned criteria can improve finite-capacity flow post-training. AFD uses these ideas for teacher distillation rather than generic reward maximization: feedback comes from co-evolving teacherstudent discriminator evaluated on on-policy student videos. 3. Method Let 𝜋𝑇 (𝑥0 𝑦) denote black-box teacher that returns video for prompt 𝑦, and let 𝜋𝜃 denote causal autoregressive student flow model with velocity field 𝑓𝜃(𝑥𝑡, 𝑡, 𝑦). The teacher may differ from the student in architecture, capacity, latent representation, and sampling schedule. We assume no access to teacher parameters, scores, latents, or denoising trajectories; the only shared interface is the completed prompt-conditioned video. At each iteration, prompts are sampled from distribution 𝑦 Y, the teacher returns 𝑥𝑇 0 𝜋𝑇 ( 𝑦), and the current student produces an on-policy rollout ˆ𝑥0 𝜋𝜃( 𝑦) under autoregressive self-conditioning. As shown in Figure 2, AFD consists of an adaptive video discriminator that estimates teacherstudent distributional discrepancy on student rollouts and DiffusionNFT update that transfers this sample-level signal to the students forward noising process. 3.1. Adaptive TeacherStudent Discrimination We train prompt-conditioned spatiotemporal discriminator 𝐷𝜙(𝑥0, 𝑦) that scores how likely video 𝑥0 is to be teacher sample given prompt 𝑦. For each prompt, we treat the teacher sample as preferred 3 On-Policy Adversarial Flow Distillation for Autoregressive Video Generation Figure 2 Overview of AFD for black-box on-policy video distillation. sampling-only teacher provides prompt-conditioned completed videos, while the current AR student provides on-policy rollouts. coevolving discriminator estimates teacherstudent distributional discrepancy from completed samples, and DiffusionNFT converts this black-box sample-level signal into dense forward-process flow updates on the students own noised states. over the current student rollout, yielding the BradleyTerry (BT) loss: L𝐷 (𝜙) := 𝔼(𝑥𝑇 0 ,ˆ𝑥0,𝑦) log 𝜎(cid:0)𝐷𝜙(𝑥𝑇 0 , 𝑦) 𝐷𝜙(ˆ𝑥0, 𝑦)(cid:1) , (1) where 𝜎() is the logistic function. This pairwise objective is standard in preference modeling: it increases the discriminator margin between teacher samples and current student samples under the same prompt, without requiring calibrated absolute rewards. In practice, 𝐷𝜙 can be initialized from video preference or text-video alignment model such as VideoAlign [Liu et al., 2025b] and adapted with LoRA. Rather than defining fixed global reward, the discriminator estimates the current discrepancy between teacher and student samples under the shared prompt distribution, providing on-the-fly feedback for on-policy rollouts when teacher scores are unavailable. The discriminator induces discrepancy signal, which we use as an adaptive reward function: 𝑟𝜙(ˆ𝑥0, 𝑦) = sg (cid:0)𝐷𝜙(ˆ𝑥0, 𝑦) 𝑏( 𝑦)(cid:1) , (2) where sg() denotes stop-gradient and 𝑏( 𝑦) is batch or prompt-level baseline. Unlike fixed reward model, this signal co-evolves with the student model and measures whether current student videos remain distinguishable from teacher videos under the current prompt distribution. 3.2. Diffusion-Native On-Policy Update The discriminator signal is sample-level because it scores completed videos. Directly optimizing this signal with reverse-trajectory policy gradients would require treating the reverse denoising chain as an RL trajectory, which is costly for long, high-resolution videos. We instead adopt forward-process diffusion optimization method that optimizes diffusion models using only clean samples and the 4 On-Policy Adversarial Flow Distillation for Autoregressive Video Generation forward noising process [Choi et al., 2026], namely DiffusionNFT [Zheng et al., 2025]. For student rollout ˆ𝑥0, timestep 𝑡 [0, 1], and noise 𝜖 (0, 𝐼), define the forward-noised sample and its corresponding flow-matching target as 𝑥𝑡 = 𝛼𝑡 ˆ𝑥0 + 𝜎𝑡𝜖, 𝑣 = (cid:164)𝛼𝑡 ˆ𝑥0 + (cid:164)𝜎𝑡𝜖. (3) We use the discriminator to score on-policy rollouts with 𝜋𝜃. We then determine the weights for positive and negative policy optimization. Concretely, for minibatch of student videos {ˆ𝑥0,𝑖}𝐵 𝑖=1, let 𝐴𝑖 = 𝑟𝜙(ˆ𝑥0,𝑖, 𝑦𝑖) and normalize it to weight 𝑤𝑖 [0, 1] within the batch. We apply the forward process on this batch of videos following Equation 3 to obtain noised samples {𝑥𝑡,𝑖}𝐵 𝑖=1. Denoting the parametrized velocity as 𝑣𝜃(𝑥𝑡,𝑖, 𝑡, 𝑦𝑖), we define two operators for positive and negative policy optimization under the on-policy sampling setting: 𝑣+ 𝜃 := (1 𝛽) sg(𝑣𝜃) + 𝛽𝑣𝜃, 𝑣 𝜃 := (1 + 𝛽) sg(𝑣𝜃) 𝛽𝑣𝜃. the negative-aware fine-tuning (NFT) loss whose optimum implicitly drives 𝑣𝜃 towards 𝑣+ is LNFT(𝜃) = 𝔼 𝑡, 𝑥𝑡,𝑖 𝐵 (cid:2) 𝑖=1 𝑤𝑖 𝑣+ 𝜃 (𝑥𝑡,𝑖, 𝑡, 𝑦𝑖) 𝑣2 + (1 𝑤𝑖) 𝑣 𝜃 (𝑥𝑡,𝑖, 𝑡, 𝑦𝑖) 𝑣2(cid:3), (4) Additionally, we include regularization term in the objective function to avoid catastrophic forgetting 𝑖=1 𝑤𝑖 𝑣𝜃(𝑥𝑡,𝑖, 𝑡, 𝑦𝑖) 𝑣ref(𝑥𝑡,𝑖, 𝑡, 𝑦𝑖)2, where the reference model 𝑣ref is the initial preLprior = 𝔼 𝑡, 𝑥𝑡,𝑖 trained diffusion model. The objective of our method AFD is finally defined as: (cid:205)𝐵 LAFD(𝜃) := LNFT(𝜃) + 𝜆priorLprior(𝜃). (5) 3.3. Black-Box Teacher Interface AFD is motivated by the measurability constraint imposed by black-box video teacher. The teacher exposes only the clean-sample channel O𝑇 : 𝑦 𝑥𝑇 0 𝜋𝑇 ( 𝑦), (6) not 𝑥 log 𝑝𝑇 (𝑥𝑡 𝑦), teacher latents, teacher reverse states, or teacher transition kernel. Any admissible distillation signal must therefore be function of completed teacher videos and completed student videos. This constraint is restrictive for an AR flow student, because the trained object is not video classifier but vector field 𝑓𝜃(𝑥𝑡, 𝑡, ℎ𝑘, 𝑦) evaluated at noised states along student-induced histories ℎ𝑘 = 𝑥 (<𝑘) 0 Let 𝑥0 = [𝑥 (1) 0 ] denote sequence of video blocks. The AR student induces , . . . , 𝑥 ( 𝐾 ) 0 . 𝜋𝜃(𝑥0 𝑦) = (cid:16) 𝜋𝜃 𝑥 (𝑘) 0 𝑥 (<𝑘) 0 (cid:17) , , 𝑦 𝐾 (cid:214) 𝑘=1 (7) so student errors alter the future conditioning distribution. bidirectional black-box teacher, however, is observed only through 𝜋𝑇 (𝑥0 𝑦). Its hidden sampler may contain 𝑀𝑇 denoising evaluations, whereas the student may use 𝑀𝑆 𝑀𝑇 . Without teacher trajectories or shared latent space, there is no observable alignment operator A𝑚,𝑠 : 𝑧𝑇 𝑚 (ℎ𝑘, 𝑥𝑆 𝑠 ) (8) from teacher step 𝑚 to student AR denoising state 𝑠. The absence of such an operator clarifies the limitations of two common baselines. SFT trains under teacher-induced prefixes rather than the 5 On-Policy Adversarial Flow Distillation for Autoregressive Video Generation students rollout distribution, so exposure bias accumulates along the video horizon [Bengio et al., 2015]. DMD-style objectives require either teacher score or teacher density ratio at the students noised state, 𝑥 log 𝑝𝑇 (𝑥𝑡 𝑦) or log . (9) 𝜋𝑇 (ˆ𝑥0 𝑦) 𝜋𝜃(ˆ𝑥0 𝑦) The score term is unobservable, and the clean-sample ratio can only be estimated from samples at 𝑥0. The relevant objective is therefore not to reconstruct the teachers hidden diffusion path, but to transfer completed-video evidence to the students own noised states. DiffusionNFT matches this interface: it requires only clean samples from the current policy, scalar score on those samples, and the students known forward noising kernel. This differs from GRPO-style diffusion RL. Methods such as Flow-GRPO [Liu et al., 2025a] and DanceGRPO [Xue et al., 2025] make diffusion or flow policies amenable to online RL by casting the reverse denoising process as an MDP, introducing stochastic reverse trajectories, and optimizing group-relative policy objectives on sampled rollouts. This interface is well suited to reward-model alignment, where the optimized models reverse sampler defines the environment. It is less suitable for black-box teacher distillation: the teacher provides no reverse actions, no teacher trajectory probabilities, and no step-level supervision on the students reverse chain. Applying reverse-MDP objective therefore introduces synthetic credit-assignment layer that is not supported by the teacher interface. AFD instead keeps the teacher-dependent signal at the clean-sample level, where it is identifiable, and uses the forward noising kernel to induce the dense denoising-time structure. 3.4. Forward-Process Credit Assignment AFD follows this design by separating teacher evidence extraction from denoising-time credit assignment. First, the discriminator estimates the density-ratio information identifiable from black-box clean samples. The optimal discriminator that minimizes the BT loss satisfies 𝜌 𝜙(ˆ𝑥0, 𝑦) := log 𝜙(ˆ𝑥0, 𝑦) 𝐷 1 𝐷 𝜙(ˆ𝑥0, 𝑦) = log 𝜋𝑇 (ˆ𝑥0 𝑦) 𝜋𝜃(ˆ𝑥0 𝑦) . (10) This ratio is defined on completed videos sampled from the current student distribution; it requires no teacher score, teacher timestep, or architectural alignment. Second, AFD converts this clean-sample ratio into vector-field target by applying the students forward noising map. Let F𝑡 denote this teacher-agnostic forward-noising operator. The key compatibility condition is that F𝑡 is entirely student-side: it depends on the students noising schedule and generated clean video, not on any teacher state. generic sample-level advantage 𝑟(ˆ𝑥0, 𝑦) defines the tilted clean-video law following DiffusionNFT: 𝜋+(ˆ𝑥0 𝑦) 𝑟(ˆ𝑥0, 𝑦)𝜋𝜃(ˆ𝑥0 𝑦). (11) Applying the students forward noising kernel 𝑝(𝑥𝑡 ˆ𝑥0) following Eq. 3 to this tilted clean distribution, it can induce corresponding distribution over noisy states according to Bayes Rule: 𝜋+ 𝑡 (𝑥𝑡 𝑦) 𝔼ˆ𝑥0𝜋𝜃 ( 𝑦) [𝑟(ˆ𝑥0, 𝑦) 𝑝(𝑥𝑡 ˆ𝑥0)] . (12) Then the optimal positive flow-matching vector field for these noised marginals is the conditional average forward velocity, 𝑣+(𝑥𝑡, 𝑡, 𝑦) = 𝔼ˆ𝑥0𝜋+ ( 𝑦), 𝑝(𝑥𝑡 ˆ𝑥0 ) [𝑣 𝑥𝑡, 𝑦] . where 𝑣 is the forward-path velocity in Eq. 3. Thus, sample-level reward over completed videos induces dense denoising-time vector-field target after being propagated through the students (13) 6 On-Policy Adversarial Flow Distillation for Autoregressive Video Generation forward process. This identity provides the forward-noising bridge: sample-level preferences define denoising-time vector field after being propagated through F𝑡. The black-box restriction and AFD therefore operate on compatible information structures: all teacher-dependent information is measurable from completed videos, while denoising-time structure is induced by the students forward process. AFD converts completed-video teacher evidence into dense corrections on the students own noised states without reconstructing the teachers hidden trajectory or storing the students reverse trajectory. Appendix 10 provides further theoretical derivation. 3.5. Training Procedure The optimization alternates between on-policy data collection, discriminator updates, and student velocity-field updates, as summarized in Algorithm 1. We implement the prior regularizer Lprior(𝜃) as velocity-regression penalty against frozen reference student to preserve the students base capabilities. Algorithm 1 Adversarial Flow Distillation (AFD) Input: Teacher API 𝜋𝑇 , Student Flow Model 𝑓𝜃, Discriminator 𝐷𝜙, Prompt distribution Output: Post-trained Student Model 𝑓𝜃 Initialize student parameters 𝜃 and EMA parameters 𝜃 𝜃, and keep 𝑓ref frozen Initialize discriminator parameters 𝜙 (e.g., via LoRA on VideoAlign) while not converged do 0 and ˆ𝑥0 0 𝜋𝑇 ( 𝑦) // 1. On-Policy Data Collection Sample batch of prompts 𝑦 Query teacher for target videos: 𝑥𝑇 Sample student rollouts: ˆ𝑥0 𝜋𝜃( 𝑦) // 2. Adaptive Teacher-Student Discrimination Compute discriminator loss L𝐷 (𝜙) comparing 𝑥𝑇 Update discriminator 𝜙 𝜙 𝜂𝐷𝜙L𝐷 (𝜙) Compute baseline 𝑏( 𝑦) (e.g., batch mean of 𝐷𝜙(ˆ𝑥0, 𝑦)) Compute advantage scores 𝑟𝜙(ˆ𝑥0, 𝑦) = sg(𝐷𝜙(ˆ𝑥0, 𝑦) 𝑏( 𝑦)) // 3. EMA Update Update target network 𝜃 𝛽 𝜃 + (1 𝛽)𝜃 4. Experiments 4.1. Setup Experimental setup. We evaluate AFD on two causal autoregressive student families, Self-Forcing and Causal-Forcing. The teacher is Seedance 2.0 [ByteDance Seed, 2026], accessed only as promptconditioned video sampling API. The main experiment is continual adaptation setting: pretrained AR student is adapted to physics-oriented target domain while preserving general video quality. We sample 200 examples from the VideoPhy-2 physics benchmark and query the teacher on the 7 On-Policy Adversarial Flow Distillation for Autoregressive Video Generation same prompts to obtain black-box adaptation videos. Unless otherwise specified, discriminators are initialized from VideoAlign [Liu et al., 2025b], adapted with LoRA, and updated online against the current student. We report VBench [Huang et al., 2024] dimensions grouped into Physics and General categories. Physics includes temporal flickering, motion smoothness, dynamic degree, human action, and spatial relationship; General is the mean over the remaining VBench dimensions. We also evaluate target-domain adaptation with VideoAlign Motion Quality (VideoAlign-MQ) [Liu et al., 2025b] and VideoPhy-2 Physical Consistency (VideoPhy-2-PC) [Bansal et al., 2026]. Full hyperparameters are provided in Appendix 7. Baselines. We consider four baselines: Base: the pretrained AR student before teacher adaptation. SFT: supervised fine-tuning on teacher-generated videos without on-policy student rollouts. GAN: adversarial video-level training with the teacherstudent discriminator, excluding the forward-process policy update. Score-free DMD: DMD-style training scaffold with the score-based distribution-matching term removed, isolating sample-level supervision under the same black-box access constraints. 4.2. Main Results Tables 1 and 2 show that AFD improves physics-sensitive generation under limited target-domain data while preserving general video generation capability. On Self-Forcing, AFD reaches the best Physics VBench Total (87.55), VideoAlign-MQ (0.605), and VideoPhy-2-PC (4.20), with General Total close to the best baseline (60.83 vs. 60.95). On Causal-Forcing, AFD gives the best Physics VBench Total (88.52) and VideoPhy-2-PC (4.24), ties the best VideoAlign-MQ (0.661), and keeps the General Total close to the highest score (59.83 vs. 60.32). Compared with the adapted baselines, AFD gives the strongest dynamic degree on both student families, indicating better motion and physical behavior without large drop in general quality. Table 1 Main VBench results after black-box continual adaptation. Physics reports all dimensions and their average; General reports the group average. Physics (VBench) General (VBench) Model Method Total Temporal Flick. Motion Smooth. Dynamic Degree Human Action Spatial Rel. Self-Forcing Causal-Forcing Base SFT GAN DMD AFD Base SFT GAN DMD AFD 68.49 58.69 83.83 81.88 87.55 86.76 76.24 83.07 82.52 88.52 98.27 96.64 99.28 99.01 98. 98.20 99.31 98.58 99.16 98.41 97.19 97.40 98.58 98.95 98.30 97.90 99.05 98.08 98.75 97.89 91.67 44.44 61.11 52.78 79.17 87.50 29.17 66.67 58.33 88.89 46.00 34.00 79.00 81.00 80. 76.00 78.00 76.00 77.00 80.00 9.32 20.97 81.18 77.66 81.50 74.20 75.69 76.00 79.35 77.39 Total 36.51 37.62 60.95 59.98 60.83 59.03 59.40 59.44 60.32 59. Figure 3 supports the trends in Tables 1 and 2. Across both AR student families, AFD keeps the main prompt content and visual quality while improving motion-related behavior. This matches the method design: the discriminator scores completed on-policy rollouts, and the DiffusionNFT update transfers this signal to the students forward-noised states. The examples therefore support the quantitative gains in dynamic degree, motion quality, and physical consistency. Additional examples are provided 8 On-Policy Adversarial Flow Distillation for Autoregressive Video Generation Table 2 Physics evaluations after small-sample physics-domain adaptation. Model Method Motion Quality Physical Consistency Self-Forcing Causal-Forcing Base SFT GAN DMD AFD Base SFT GAN DMD AFD 0.341 0.296 0.541 0.420 0.605 0.520 0.499 0.582 0.661 0. 4.04 3.72 4.10 3.72 4.20 4.16 4.17 4.16 4.14 4.24 Figure 3 Qualitative visualizations of AFD in the main experiment. The first row shows Self-Forcing examples, and the second row shows Causal-Forcing examples. Each example shows representative teacherstudent comparison frames under the same prompt, illustrating how the on-policy AFD update changes the students generated video distribution. Prompts are given in Table 4. in Appendix 8. 4.3. Ablations Continual learning on stylized domain. We further test AFD on shifted visual domain using 200 Disney-style animation prompts. Figure 4 shows that the adapted student follows the new style while preserving prompt alignment and coherent motion. This suggests that AFD is not limited to the physics prompt distribution used in the main experiment. It supports continual learning by using the teacherstudent discriminator to provide feedback on the current target domain and the forward-process update to transfer this feedback to the students denoising states, without teacher scores or architectural alignment. Discriminator learning rate. Because AFDs supervision is generated by co-evolving discriminator, the discriminator update rate 𝜂𝐷 directly controls how informative the students reward signal is over training. We sweep 𝜂𝐷 {0, 1106, 5106, 1105, 5105} with all other hyperparameters 9 On-Policy Adversarial Flow Distillation for Autoregressive Video Generation Figure 4 Qualitative examples of AFD on Disney-style animation dataset for continual learning. Detailed prompts are provided in Table 5. fixed, and track the discriminator reward 𝑟𝜙 on student rollouts. Figure 5 shows that both under-updating and over-updating the discriminator degrade the quality of the rollout reward. When 𝜂𝐷 1106, the discriminator lags behind the evolving student distribution: 𝑟𝜙 rapidly approaches 1, indicating saturation of stale scoring function rather than reliable reduction of the teacherstudent gap. When 𝜂𝐷 = 5105, the discriminator becomes overly strong early in training; 𝑟𝜙 remains near 0 across the batch, reducing the contrast between positive and negative rollout weights and weakening the student update. Intermediate rates (5106 and 1105) avoid these saturation regimes and produce gradual increase in 𝑟𝜙, which is consistent with the intended role of 𝐷𝜙 as an adaptive estimator of the current teacherstudent discrepancy rather than fixed reward model. Discriminator loss. We next replace the Bradley Terry discriminator objective from Section 3 with GAN-style binary classification loss while keeping the same on-policy data, discriminator architecture, and forward-process student update. Figure 6 compares the resulting VBench motion and physics dimensions. BT improves dynamic degree by 9.72 points over the GAN loss (88.89 vs. 79.17) and slightly improves motion smoothness (98.89 vs. 98.53), while temporal flickering, human action, and spatial relation remain effectively tied. Figure 5 Discriminator learning-rate ablation. The rollout reward 𝑟𝜙 is most informative at intermediate 𝜂𝐷; overly small or large update rates lead to reward hacking or suppressed learning. This pattern matches the role of the discriminator in AFD. The student update does not need globally calibrated real/fake probability; it needs reliable on-policy ranking signal for forming positive and negative rollouts in the forward-process objective. The BT loss compares teacher and student videos under the same prompt and optimizes the margin 𝐷𝜙(𝑥𝑇 0 , 𝑦) 𝐷𝜙(ˆ𝑥0, 𝑦), so prompt difficulty, content category, and static appearance biases are largely cancelled inside each pair. GAN-style loss instead trains an absolute classifier over teacher and student samples, which can be dominated by easy distributional cues and can satFigure 6 VBench ablation of the discriminator loss. Replacing the prompt-paired BradleyTerry objective with GAN-style binary loss mainly reduces dynamic degree, while the other motion and physics dimensions remain close. On-Policy Adversarial Flow Distillation for Autoregressive Video Generation urate when the discriminator becomes too confident. BT therefore provides smoother relative advantage for AFD, yielding stronger motion improvement while keeping the general video generation capability of the student. 5. Conclusion AFD suggests that black-box video distillation should be formulated around the information interface available to the student, rather than around the teachers hidden generation process. For causal AR students, the missing ingredient is not access to the teacher trajectory, but principled way to connect observable sample-level discrepancies to the students own denoising-time states. By separating these two roles into adaptive discrimination and forward-process credit assignment, AFD turns completed-video supervision into an on-policy training signal that improves motion and physical behavior without sacrificing general quality. This perspective offers practical and extensible route for transferring capabilities from powerful sampling-only video models to efficient autoregressive generators. 6. Limitations Our experiments focus on two causal autoregressive student backbones and small set of target domains, mainly physics-oriented prompts and stylized animation prompts. Broader studies across more teachers, longer videos, and more diverse prompt distributions would further test the generality of AFD. In addition, AFD uses an online discriminator, so its performance can depend on discriminator update rate and reward calibration; our ablations provide initial guidance, but more systematic tuning study is left for future work. Evaluation on broader reasoning-oriented benchmarks such as V-ReasonBench [Luo et al., 2025] is left for future work.

Hyperparameters We provide detailed hyperparameters in Table 3. The table lists the model, optimization, diffusion, regularization, and rollout settings used for AFD training. 14 On-Policy Adversarial Flow Distillation for Autoregressive Video Generation Table 3 Comprehensive hyperparameters for AFD training. We detail the configurations used across model and video specifications, LoRA fine-tuning, optimization, diffusion process, selective regularization, and streaming rollout. Module Hyperparameter Value Model & Video Specs LoRA Fine-Tuning Optimization Selective Regularization Base Architecture Video Resolution (𝐻 𝑊) Rank (𝑟) Scaling Factor (𝛼) Dropout Rate Gradient Checkpointing Precision Mode Optimizer Learning Rate (𝜂) Weight Decay Max Gradient Norm Interpolation Strength (𝛽) Prior Loss Weight (𝜆prior) Advantage Clip Max Reward Normalization EMA Decay Rate (𝛾) Self-Forcing / Causal-Forcing 480 256 256 0.0 Enabled bfloat16 AdamW (𝛽1 = 0.999, 𝜖 = 1𝑒 8) 1e-5 1e-4 1.0 0.9, 𝛽2 = 0.1 1e-4 5.0 Global Std with Per-Prompt Tracking 0.99 8. Additional Qualitative Visualizations We provide additional AFD visualizations in img 79. These examples further illustrate the same pattern observed in the main experiments: AFD improves motion evolution and physical plausibility while preserving the prompt-level semantics and visual fidelity of the base autoregressive video student. 9. Prompts for Qualitative Visualizations We present the prompts used for the qualitative examples in img 3 and 4. 10. Theoretical Insights In this section, we provide theoretical analysis on the proposed method Adversarial Flow Distillation (AFD). We reveal the connection between policy optimization in AFD to the KL-divergence on-policy distillation from teacher. Let 𝜋𝑇 (𝑥0 𝑦) denote the teacher distribution accessible only through clean samples, and 𝜋𝜃(𝑥0 𝑦) the student parameterized by velocity field 𝑣𝜃(𝑥𝑡, 𝑦, 𝑡). Let 𝑣old(𝑥𝑡, 𝑦, 𝑡) denote the EMA data-collection policy, with induced distribution 𝜋old. The forward process is with sample-level forward velocity 𝑥𝑡 = 𝛼𝑡 𝑥0 + 𝜎𝑡𝜖, 𝜖 (0, 𝐼), 𝑣(𝑥0, 𝜖, 𝑡) = (cid:164)𝛼𝑡 𝑥0 + (cid:164)𝜎𝑡𝜖. (14) (15) 15 On-Policy Adversarial Flow Distillation for Autoregressive Video Generation Figure 7 Additional qualitative visualization of AFD. Prompt: breathtaking tightrope walker, clad in sleek black outfit and striking red helmet, navigates narrow tightrope suspended high above lush green landscape. The camera captures close-up of their focused face, framed by dark visor, as they expertly manipulate wooden block along the rope using long, slender balance pole. The block glides smoothly, creating subtle groove in the taut rope, testament to their precision and control. The background blurs into vibrant tapestry of greenery, enhancing the sense of isolation and danger. The tightrope, thin line of tension, contrasts sharply with the serene environment, while the camera remains steady, allowing the viewer to fully immerse in the walkers intense concentration and the delicate dance of balance and skill. 16 On-Policy Adversarial Flow Distillation for Autoregressive Video Generation Figure 8 Additional qualitative visualization of AFD. Prompt: majestic dragon kite soars through narrow urban canyon, framed by towering, weathered brick buildings. The kite, intricately crafted with vibrant red body and fierce, golden head, glides effortlessly against backdrop of deep blue skies, its tail adorned with cascade of colorful ribbons that flutter in the breeze. The camera captures the kites dynamic flight, showcasing its graceful turns and the subtle interplay of light and shadow as it navigates the tight space between the buildings. The scene is enhanced by soft, golden-hour glow, casting warm hue over the textured brick facades, while the kites movement creates striking contrast against the static architecture. This cinematic moment celebrates the artistry of kite flying, blending the beauty of nature with the urban landscape. 17 On-Policy Adversarial Flow Distillation for Autoregressive Video Generation Figure 9 Additional qualitative visualization of AFD. Prompt: In breathtaking underwater tableau, two swimmers glide through the crystal-clear water, their synchronized breaststroke movements creating mesmerizing dance of rhythm and grace. The camera, positioned at slight angle, captures the swimmers powerful legs and arms as they propel themselves forward, their bodies slicing through the water with precision. The first swimmer, clad in sleek black swimsuit, leads the pack, while the second, in vibrant red suit, trails closely, their synchronized movements echoing fierce competition. The waters surface shimmers with sunlight, casting dynamic reflections that enhance the scenes ethereal quality. The static camera allows for focused view of their synchronized strokes, highlighting the swimmers strength and agility, while the serene underwater environment amplifies the beauty of their synchronized performance. 18 On-Policy Adversarial Flow Distillation for Autoregressive Video Generation The discriminator 𝐷𝜙( ˆ𝑥0, 𝑦) (0, 1) is trained to distinguish teacher samples 𝑥𝑇 rollouts ˆ𝑥0 𝜋𝜃, with optimum 0 𝜋𝑇 from student 𝐷 𝜙( ˆ𝑥0, 𝑦) = 𝜋𝑇 ( ˆ𝑥0 𝑦) 𝜋𝑇 ( ˆ𝑥0 𝑦) + 𝜋𝜃( ˆ𝑥0 𝑦) , 𝜌 𝜙( ˆ𝑥0, 𝑦) := log 𝜙( ˆ𝑥0, 𝑦) 𝐷 1 𝐷 𝜙( ˆ𝑥0, 𝑦) = log 𝜋𝑇 ( ˆ𝑥0 𝑦) 𝜋𝜃( ˆ𝑥0 𝑦) . (16) In the AFD setting, if we set advantage-form reward dependent on the density ratio estimated by discriminator: Recall from DiffusionNFT Section 3.1: 𝑟𝜙(𝑥0, 𝑦) := exp (cid:0)𝜌𝜙(𝑥0, 𝑦)(cid:1) . 𝜋+(𝑥0𝑐) := 𝜋old(𝑥0𝑜 = 1, 𝑐) = 𝑟(𝑥0, 𝑐) 𝑝𝜋old (𝑜 = 1𝑐) 𝜋old(𝑥0𝑐). (17) (18) This is the conditional distribution of 𝑥0 given that the optimality variable 𝑜 equals 1 i.e., the reward-reweighted version of 𝜋old that puts more mass on high-reward samples. The tilted policy can also be written as and at optimal 𝜙 due to the on-policy setting 𝜋old = 𝜋𝜃, this reduces to 𝜋+(𝑥0 𝑦) exp (cid:0)𝜌𝜙(cid:1) 𝜋old(𝑥0 𝑦), 𝜋+(𝑥0 𝑦) 𝜋𝑇 (𝑥0). (19) (20) Equation 20 demonstrates that the tilted policy 𝜋+ under the optimal discriminator recovers the teacher policy. In the following proposition, we show that the policy optimization process in AFD pushes the policy towards the teacher model. Proposition 1. Under an optimal discriminator, the policy improvement step from 𝜋old to the reward-tilted distribution 𝜋+ is equivalent to on-policy reverse-KL distillation from the teacher 𝜋𝑇 . Proof. The tilted policy 𝜋+ is the solution to the optimization problem: 𝔼𝑥0𝜋 [𝜌(𝑥0, 𝑦)] KL(cid:16) 𝜋 𝜋old(cid:17) . max 𝜋 Plugging the optimal discriminator into 𝜌, we obtain that the optimization problem becomes max 𝜋 max 𝜋 max 𝜋 KL(cid:16) 𝜋 𝜋old(cid:17) (cid:20) log (cid:21) 𝜋𝑇 (𝑥0) 𝜋𝜃(𝑥0) 𝔼𝑥0𝜋 KL(𝜋 𝜋𝑇 ) + KL(𝜋 𝜋𝜃) KL(cid:16) KL(𝜋 𝜋𝑇 ) 𝜋 𝜋old(cid:17) Equation 24 finally becomes reverse-KL distillation from the teacher model. (21) (22) (23) (24) On-Policy Adversarial Flow Distillation for Autoregressive Video Generation Table 4 Prompts used for Figure 3. Example physics1 physics2 physics physics4 Prompt In breathtaking display of aquatic athleticism, skilled skier glides effortlessly across the shimmering surface of tranquil lake, propelled by powerful ski jet. The scene opens with the skier, clad in vibrant red and black wetsuit, gracefully poised on the water, their skis slicing through the glassy surface. The ski jet, sleek black vessel with striking red stripe, glides alongside, its engine humming with precision as it maintains steady pace. The camera captures the dynamic interplay between the skier and the jet, showcasing the skiers agility and balance as they lean into the turns, their movements fluid and controlled. The backdrop features serene landscape of lush greenery and distant mountains, enhancing the tranquil ambiance. As the skier gains momentum, the camera remains fixed, allowing viewers to appreciate the exhilarating dance of speed and skill on the water, culminating in thrilling display of aquatic prowess. In serene bathroom setting, person clad in light blue shirt and dark pants stands before sleek white sink, the soft glow of natural light filtering through nearby window. The camera captures close-up of their hands, one gently squeezing vibrant blue toothpaste tube, while the other holds pristine white toothbrush, poised to receive the creamy substance. As the tube is squeezed, steady stream of toothpaste flows onto the bristles, creating mesmerizing cascade of blue that contrasts beautifully with the white brush. The scene is bathed in warm, inviting light, enhancing the textures of the toothpaste and the brush, while the background remains softly blurred, drawing the viewers focus to the meticulous act of oral hygiene. The static camera angle ensures clear view of the process, capturing the rhythmic motion of the hands and the satisfying result of perfectly coated toothbrush. In serene, softly lit room, pair of hands gracefully manipulates knitting needles, their rhythmic movements creating mesmerizing dance of craftsmanship. The camera captures close-up of the hands, framed against blurred backdrop, enhancing the focus on the intricate process. The needles glisten under the warm, diffused light, casting gentle shadows that accentuate the texture of the yarn.
